For the details of its jurisprudential and theological discussions, refer to what has been authored by the masters of the art among the ancients and the moderns, especially the luminaries of the present age among the investigative researchers of proofs.

## (Scholarly Discussion)

The bond of lineage (رابطة النسب)—which is the bond that links one human individual to another from the perspective of birth and the commonality of the womb—is originally a natural, ontological (تكوينية) bond that forms peoples and tribes. It carries the traits arising from the blood, transmitting them according to the transmission of the blood. It is the principle for national manners, customs, and traditions, as it mixes and blends with other influential causes and factors.

Human societies, both advanced and unadvanced, have a kind of regard for it in social traditions and laws in general: in marriage, inheritance, and so forth. Despite this, they continue to manipulate this lineage bond, expanding or narrowing it according to the interests arising from the specificities of their society. As you heard in the previous discussions, most of the past nations did not formally recognize the kinship of women, while they recognized the kinship and sonship of the adopted child (الدعي). Similarly, Islam negates kinship between the warring disbeliever and the Muslim, and attributes the child to the [marital] bed (الولد للفراش), and so on.

When Islam recognized kinship for women by giving them full partnership in wealth and full freedom in will and action—as you heard in the previous discussions—and thereby the son and the daughter became equal in the degree of formal kinship and womb-relationship, and likewise the father and the mother, the brother and the sister, the grandfather and the grandmother, the paternal uncle and aunt, and the maternal uncle and aunt, the formal pillar of lineage began to descend from the side of the daughters just as it used to descend from the side of the sons. Thus, the daughter's son became a son to the person, just like the sonship of the son's son, and so on downwards. Likewise, the son's daughter and the daughter's daughter became two daughters to the person equally. Upon this, the rulings in marriages and inheritances were established. You have already learned that the verse of prohibition: **حُرِّمَتْ عَلَيْكُمْ أُمَّهاتُكُمْ وَبَناتُكُمْ** indicates this.

The predecessors among our researchers fell short in this matter and its likes (which is a social and legal matter), for they considered it a linguistic matter in which one could rest upon the judgment of the language. Thus, the dispute intensified among them regarding what the word "son" (الابن), for example, was coined for—some generalizing and some specifying. All of that is an error.

Some of them mentioned: "What the language knows of sonship is what proceeds from the side of the son. As for the daughter's son and everything that proceeds from her side, because these are affiliated with their fathers and not their maternal grandfather, the Arabs do not count them as sons of the person. As for the saying of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding the two Hasans: 'These two sons of mine are Imams, whether they stand or sit,' and other such statements, this usage is an honorific usage (إطلاق تشريفي)." He recited in that regard the saying of the poet:

*Our sons are the sons of our sons, but our daughters—*
*Their sons are the sons of distant men.*

Similar to it is the saying of another:

*The mothers of people are but vessels,*
*Entrusted [with seed], while lineages belong to fathers.*

I say: The path of inquiry became confused for him, as he thought it was a linguistic inquiry, imagining that if the Arabs had coined the word "son" to include the daughter's son, the result of the inquiry would have changed. This is an oversight of the fact that the effects and rulings established in different human societies regarding fatherhood, sonship, and the like do not follow languages; rather, they follow the type of social structure and the traditions prevalent therein. These rulings and effects may change by changing the social tradition in the society while the language remains as it is. This reveals that the inquiry is social (اجتماعي) or related to it, not verbal or linguistic.

As for the poetry he recited, poetry is worth nothing in the market of truths—it is nothing but an imaginative decoration and an illusory embellishment—such that one would argue with everything said by a frivolous poet, especially in matters where the Quran intervenes, which is a decisive word and not a jest.

As for the issue of sons being affiliated with their fathers rather than their grandfathers from the mothers' side, besides not being a verbal or linguistic issue, it is not among the branches of lineage such that the affiliation of the son and daughter to the father would necessitate the severance of their lineage from the mother's side. Rather, it is among the branches of the man's guardianship (قيمومة) over the household in terms of maintenance, the raising of children, and the like.

In short, the mother transmits the bond of lineage to her children, whether male or female, just as the father transmits it. Among its prominent effects in Islam are inheritance and the prohibition of marriage. Yes, there are other rulings and issues that have specific criteria, such as the affiliation of the child, maintenance, and the issue of the share of the "near of kin" (أولي القربى) among the descendants of the Prophet (السادات), and each follows its own specific criterion.

## (Another Scholarly Discussion)

Marriage and union are among the social traditions that have never ceased to be prevalent in human societies—whatever society it may be—according to the history of this species available to us to this day. This in itself is evidence of it being a primordial tradition (سنة فطرية).

Furthermore, among the strongest evidences for this is that the male and the female are equipped, according to their physical structure, with the means of reproduction and procreation, as we have mentioned repeatedly. Both groups (male and female) are equal in seeking that, although the female is additionally provided with the apparatus of nursing and the primordial emotions (العواطف الفطرية) suitable for raising children.

Then, there are human instincts that incline toward the love of children, accept the judgment of nature that the human being continues through the continuation of his offspring, acknowledge that the woman is a repose (سكن) for the man and vice versa, and respect the principle of inheritance after respecting the principle of ownership and exclusivity, and respect the necessity of establishing a household.

Societies that respect these primordial principles and rulings in general have no choice but to acknowledge the tradition of marriage in a way that involves exclusivity in some sense—meaning that men and women do not mix in a way that invalidates lineages, even if one assumes protection against the corruption of general health and the strength of procreation caused by the prevalence of adultery and indecency.

These are principles recognized by all nations that follow the tradition of marriage in general, whether they restrict one to one, or permit many women for one man or vice versa, or many of them for many of them, despite the variation of these traditions among nations. For they nevertheless consider marriage by its specific characteristic, which is a type of companionship and association between the spouses.

Thus, indecency (الفحشاء) and profligacy (السفاح) which sever the lineage and corrupt the ancestry are the first things loathed by the human primordial nature (الفطرة) which judges in favor of marriage. You continue to see traces of this loathing among different nations and diverse societies, even those nations that live in total freedom for men and women in sexual relations and mixing; for they are repulsed by these unrestrained abandonments, and you see them living by laws that preserve for them the rulings of lineages in some way.

The human being, while acknowledging the tradition of marriage, is not restricted in it according to his nature; he does not forbid himself a relative or a stranger, nor does the male human being avoid a mother, a sister, a daughter, or others, nor does the female avoid a father, a brother, or a son, according to the sexual drive. History and reports establish the marriage of mothers, sisters, daughters, and others in great, advanced nations as well as lowly ones. Reports confirm the adultery prevalent in civilized nations today between brothers and sisters, fathers and daughters, and others. For the tyranny of lust is not stood up to by anything. What existed among these nations of avoiding the marriage of mothers, sisters, daughters, and those who follow them was only an inherited tradition that perhaps ended in some national manners and customs.

If you compare the laws legislated in Islam to organize the matter of union with other laws and traditions prevalent in the world, and you contemplate them fairly, you will find them more precise and more guaranteed for all aspects of precaution in preserving lineages and other primordial human interests. All the rulings it has legislated regarding the matter of marriage and what follows it return to the preservation of lineages and the blocking of the path to adultery.

That which has taken into account the interest of preserving lineages without an intermediary is the prohibition of marrying married women (المحصنات). By this, the union of a woman with more than one husband at the same time is abolished, for in it is the corruption of lineages, just as it is the criterion for establishing the waiting period of divorce (عدة الطلاق) by the woman waiting for three menstrual cycles to guard against the mixing of waters.

As for the other categories of women whose marriage is prohibited—which are the fourteen categories enumerated in the verses of prohibition—the criterion in prohibiting their marriage is the blocking of the door to adultery. For the human being—within the domestic society—most frequently associates, mixes, is unrestrained, and continues in companionship with these fourteen categories. The continuity of companionship and the touch of unrestraint necessitate the complete turning of the soul and the focus of thought toward them, in a way that leads to the awakening of animal inclinations and emotions and the agitation of the drives of lust, and its driving the human being toward what his nature finds pleasurable and his soul yearns for. "He who hovers around the sanctuary is likely to fall into it."

Thus, it was necessary not to limit the matter to the mere prohibition of adultery in these cases. For the continuity of companionship, the repeated attack of the soul's whisperings, and the arrival of one concern after another would not leave the human being room to maintain a single prohibition against adultery.

Rather, it was necessary that these be prohibited eternally, and that religious upbringing be established upon this until a total despair of reaching them or attaining them settles in the hearts. This kills the attachment of lust to them, cuts off its source, and uproots it from its origin. This is what we see from many Muslims, even among those deeply involved in indecency and unrestrained in reprehensible acts, that the thought of indecency with the prohibited degrees (المحارم) and the violation of the sanctity of mothers and daughters does not cross their minds. Were it not for that, hardly a house would be free from the indecency of adultery and the like.

This is just as Islam blocked the door to adultery with those other than the prohibited degrees by mandating the veil (الحجاب) and prohibiting the mixing of men with women and women with men. Were it not for that, the prohibition of adultery would not have succeeded in partitioning the human being from this heinous act. Thus, there is one of two matters: either the prohibition of mixing, as in one group, or the settling of despair from attaining them altogether through an eternal prohibition upon which the human being is raised until he is firm in this belief—he does not see its likeness in what he sees, nor hears it in what he hears, so it never crosses his mind at all.

The confirmation of this is found in the current state of Western nations. These Christian societies used to maintain the prohibition of adultery (الزنا) and considered polygamy to be akin to it. However, they permitted the free mixing of women and men, and it was not long before indecency (الفحشاء) spread among them to such an extent that one could hardly find one in a thousand who is safe from this malady. Nor is there one in a thousand among their men who can be certain that the children attributed to him are truly from his own loins. This disease did not stop there; it spread to men with their unmarriageable kin (المحارم) — sisters, daughters, and mothers — then to relations between men and boys, then among the youths themselves, and so on and so forth. The matter has reached a point where this group [women], whom Allah, the Glorified, created only as a source of tranquility (سكن) for humanity and a blessing by which the backbone of the human race is sustained and the life of the species is made pleasant, has become a lure used for trapping in every political, economic, and social affair, and a means to attain every objective that corrupts the life of the individual and society. Human life has turned into a mere imaginary wish, and into play and diversion (لعبا ولهوا) in the full sense of the word, until the rent has become too wide for the patcher to mend.

This is the principle upon which Islam based the prohibition of the forbidden categories (المحرمات) — whether those whose prohibition is absolute (المبهمات) or others — in the chapter of marriage, with the exception of married women (المحصنات), as you have come to know.

The effect of this ruling in preventing the spread of adultery and its infiltration into the domestic society (المجتمع المنزلي) is like the effect of the ruling of the veil (الحجاب) in preventing the appearance of adultery and the spread of corruption in civil society (المجتمع المدني), as you have understood.

It has been mentioned previously that His saying, the Exalted: **وَرَبَائِبُكُمُ اللَّاتِي فِي حُجُورِكُم** — "and your stepdaughters who are under your care" [Qarai 4:23] — is not devoid of an allusion to this wisdom. It is also possible that the allusion to this is found in His saying, the Exalted, at the end of the verses of prohibition:

**يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ أَن يُخَفِّفَ عَنكُمْ ۚ وَخُلِقَ الْإِنسَانُ ضَعِيفًا** — "Allah desires to lighten your burden, for man was created weak" [Qarai 4:28]. For the definitive prohibition of these fourteen categories by Allah, the Glorified, lifts from the shoulders of man the heavy burden of having to endure patience against desiring them, inclining toward them, or attaining them when such a thing would otherwise be possible. Man was created weak (ضعيفا) in the face of psychological inclinations and sensual motives, and He, the Exalted, has said: **إِنَّ كَيْدَكُنَّ عَظِيمٌ** — "Your stratagems are indeed great" [Qarai 12:28]. Indeed, it is a matter requiring immense patience for a man to live with one or more non-mahram women, accompanying them in private and in public, interacting with them night and day, while his ears and eyes are constantly filled with their subtle gestures and sweet movements time after time, and then to remain patient against what his soul whispers to him regarding them, not responding to what it yearns for. For the primary needs are two: food and marriage; everything else is secondary and returns to these two. It is as if this is what he صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم alluded to in his saying: **من تزوج أحرز نصف دينه ـ فليتق الله في النصف الآخر** — "Whoever marries has secured half of his religion, so let him be God-conscious (فليتق الله) regarding the other half."
